D8 AnthropologyL2 UnderstandReference

Sepia — Cuttlefish Ink and Eumelanin

The brown ink Sepia officinalis squirts to evade predators dries to a stable drawing medium; the chromophore is eumelanin, the same polymer as in human skin and hair

Cuttlefish ink is a colloidal suspension of eumelanin granules. Eumelanin is a heterogeneous polymer of 5,6-dihydroxyindole — its broad-band absorption gives the warm dark-brown sepia tone. From Greco-Roman scribes through the 19th century, sepia was a primary drawing-ink choice.

Drag concentration; thin washes give cool warm browns, thick ink approaches near-black.
